title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who describe their approach to staying healthy. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Body and Mind: Four People on Health and Fitness

texts.A:
### Stella, 33, Accountant
[43]I got serious about fitness after a doctor told me my blood pressure was dangerously high for someone my age.[/43] That was the wake-up call I needed. I joined a gym, started cooking at home more, and cut out alcohol completely for six months. [47]The biggest change I noticed wasn't physical – it was my mental state.[/47] I felt clearer, calmer, and more in control of my life. I've kept most of the habits going since then, although I allow myself the occasional treat without guilt. [51]I've also started a weekly yoga class which I initially found deeply boring but now genuinely look forward to.[/51]
texts.B:
### Kwame, 48, Chef
You might assume that being a chef means eating well, but the reality is the opposite. [44]Working long hours in a kitchen meant I was grabbing unhealthy snacks and surviving on coffee for most of my thirties.[/44] I've made a real effort to change that. I now meal prep on my days off and prioritise sleep above everything else. [48]I've read extensively about nutrition and I think the biggest myth in the fitness world is that exercise matters more than what you eat.[/48] In my view, diet is everything. [52]I don't belong to a gym and I don't run – my exercise comes from walking everywhere and doing physical work in the kitchen.[/52]
texts.C:
### Yuki, 27, Software Developer
[45]I started cycling to work two years ago, initially just to save money on transport, and ended up losing eight kilograms without even trying.[/45] It's now my favourite part of the day. [49]I find that physical activity first thing in the morning sets a completely different tone for the rest of my day – I'm sharper, more focused, and less stressed.[/49] I cycle thirty kilometres daily regardless of weather, which my colleagues find extraordinary. I've also taken up swimming at weekends. My approach to health is fairly simple: move more, eat real food, sleep enough. I don't follow any particular diet and I think rigid rules often do more harm than good.
texts.D:
### Fatima, 56, School Counsellor
I've been through different phases with health and fitness over the years. [46]In my forties, I became obsessed with extreme diets and exercise regimes, which actually made me quite unwell both physically and emotionally.[/46] Now I take a much more balanced view. [50]I believe mental health and physical health are completely inseparable and that too many fitness plans ignore the psychological side entirely.[/50] I do gentle exercise – mainly long walks and stretching – and I focus on reducing stress through mindfulness. [53]My advice to anyone starting a fitness journey is to be patient and kind to themselves.[/53] Sustainable change comes from self-compassion, not from punishing yourself.

questions.question: was motivated to change their lifestyle by advice from a medical professional?
questions.explanation: Stella got serious about fitness after a doctor told her her blood pressure was dangerously high.

questions.question: admits that their profession made it harder, not easier, to maintain healthy habits?
questions.explanation: Kwame says working long hours as a chef meant surviving on coffee and unhealthy snacks.

questions.question: began an activity for a practical reason unrelated to fitness, but gained health benefits?
questions.explanation: Yuki started cycling to work to save money on transport but ended up losing weight as a result.

questions.question: acknowledges that an earlier, more extreme approach to fitness was harmful?
questions.explanation: Fatima says her obsession with extreme diets and exercise in her forties made her unwell.

questions.question: found the most significant improvement was in their emotional rather than physical wellbeing?
questions.explanation: Stella says the biggest change she noticed after improving her health was her mental state, not her body.

questions.question: challenges a widely held belief about which aspect of a healthy lifestyle is most important?
questions.explanation: Kwame says the biggest myth in fitness is that exercise matters more than diet, arguing diet is everything.

questions.question: finds that exercising in the morning has a positive effect on the rest of their day?
questions.explanation: Yuki says physical activity first thing in the morning makes her sharper, more focused and less stressed.

questions.question: argues that a key dimension of wellbeing is often overlooked in standard fitness advice?
questions.explanation: Fatima believes mental and physical health are inseparable but too many fitness plans ignore the psychological side.

questions.question: mentions taking up an activity they initially disliked but have come to enjoy?
questions.explanation: Stella started a yoga class she found deeply boring at first but now looks forward to.

questions.question: maintains fitness without using dedicated sports facilities or following a formal exercise programme?
questions.explanation: Kwame doesn't belong to a gym or run – his exercise comes from walking everywhere and kitchen work.
